claims.name: arsa_caksu
claims.cite: Those who see, with the seer's eye

claims.name: perceives
claims.cite: see, with the seer's eye, things that lie beyond the senses

claims.name: not_overturned_by
claims.cite: their word is not overturned by inference

rejected_readings.label: inference_overrules_testimony
rejected_readings.rendering: The inference of ordinary reasoners can overturn the testimony of the seers.
rejected_readings.why: Directly contradicts the verse's core claim that the rsis' word cannot be overturned by inference. The commentary explicitly denies that the weaker instrument (inference) can overrule the stronger (direct seeing).

rejected_readings.label: no_seer_eye_distinction
rejected_readings.rendering: The seers have no special perceptual faculty; their knowledge comes only from ordinary inference like everyone else.
rejected_readings.why: Denies the ārṣa-cakṣus (seer's eye) as a distinct power, which is central to the verse's claim that they see what lies beyond the senses. This demotes the seer's faculty from a unique power to ordinary cognition.

rejected_readings.label: inference_equals_direct_seeing
rejected_readings.rendering: Inference and direct perception have equal epistemic status in the domain of dharma.
rejected_readings.why: Reverses the hierarchy established by the verse and commentary. The commentary is explicit that the weaker instrument (inference) does not override the stronger (direct seeing), and that direct seeing is available to seers but inference to all.

notes: The verse establishes an epistemological hierarchy: the rsis possess the ārṣa-cakṣus (seer's eye), a power of direct perception of the atīndriya (beyond senses), and their vacana (testimony) cannot be overturned by anumāna (inference), even though inference is a valid pramāṇa. The contract captures the comparative claim rather than asserting absolute knowledge—inference remains valid, merely subordinate to direct seeing in hierarchical rank.
